text: All right. Hi, everybody. Welcome to the lightning talk section. My name is Jessie. I'm going
to be giving a quick lightning talk on hallucinating the app protocol, grounding your AI agents
with the official app proto docs. If you met me last year, I was at the conference talking
about OAuth. Today I am here because I chose to build and serve for free a remote MCP server
for the app protocol documentation that is available online. I'm hoping it is a helpful
tool for developers. And you may be wondering why would somebody decide to build a remote
MCP server for the documentation? I'm hopefully going to explain. So first, if you are not familiar
with what RAG is, I'm not going to get too deep into this because it's a lightning talk,
but RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation. And you can think of retrieval augmented generation
as adding indexing to your source content. So the same way that adding a SQL index to like
a large SQL table will make querying that data more efficient, RAG makes LLM queries more efficient.
By preprocessing the source content into an index of vector embeddings, you get more efficient
retrieval of content that is specifically related to your query. So the way this works is it allows
you to combine multiple reference sources into a single index. You can have that content supersede
whatever the model was trained on. So you can have more up to date, more relevant content.
And then you can also make sure that your retrievals are getting grounded in that content. So you're
reducing hallucinations. Quickly, what is an MCP server? So if you're not familiar with MCP,
it came out in late 2024. Basically, MCP servers expose tools, prompts, and resources to your agents.
So if you are in Cloud Code or ChatGPT or whatever your like tool of choice is, you can often install
an MCP server that will expose these additional tools, prompts, and resources to your queries.
So let's compare what happens when you do a URL search to when you're using something like a
a rag instance. So if you say, I'm building an app proto app, and I want you to search the app proto
documentations for lexicons, and then you're incorporating that into your query. Basically,
the key point here, you can see this like whole little chart of what's happening. Your prompt,
your agent has to go get the docs, it has to tokenize that information, it has to process it. And
basically, the takeaway here is that everything that's going into your chat context is getting tokenized
and embedded. That that could be pre processed. And that's what the rag approach allows us to do.
So instead of your agent tokenizing and embedding all of those documentation sources,
all of that tokenization and embedding vector embedding creation has already happened. And it's
just being served by the MCP server. MCP servers come in two flavors, they come in local servers that we run
on our local machines and remote servers that are hosted somewhere in the cloud.
By essentially pre processing a rag index and then caching it and serving it in the cloud as a remote
server, you're effectively caching that tokenization process for anyone in the world who wants to consume
it. So this is why I built a app proto docs MCP server. Last year, when I was first getting into MCP
and trying to explore it and study it, I thought Cloudflare's documentation server was really
interesting to me. They had some templates I wanted to clone and see if I could play with it.
This was also in the spirit of we can just build things. So you can find these online
on GitHub at Ember MCP app proto docs and the worker. How does this actually work? There are three pieces
to my MCP server that serves the documentation. There's a cron scraper that actually goes and scrapes
the documentation sites once a week so that it's kept up to date as new documentation is added.
There's the rag index where a different model preprocesses those documentation source sites
into a rag index. And then there's the actual MCP server itself which exposes that rag index for your
agents to query it. So the cron scraper it lives at app proto docs worker. Right now there are only three
sources here that are being scraped. I'm actually making a list at this conference of sources that
people want added to this so if you have sources that you would like added or feel like should be
included please let me know. You can also open PRs on this public repo. Then we have the rag index
and I am using a tool that Cloudflare has made very affordable right now. I don't expect it to stay this
affordable forever but right now it's lovely. And so I am using the open source Quen3 model to do the
vector embedding that creates the index and that's this Cloudflare has some really easy tools that make
it very simple to do this. So that is kind of using some of their stuff. And then the MCP server itself,
mine only exposes a single tool and that tool is called search documentation.
You can make MCP servers as complicated and as interesting as you want but mine was just as
kind of a prototype so it just does search documentation. To install this in your agent what
you want to do is depends on your agent but for example in Cloud you add a JSON config which points
at the MCP server mine lives at this URL. And I was not the first nor will I be the last person to create
an MCP server for at Proto. So I wanted to highlight some of the other very awesome MCP servers that exist
in the community. I've added two more since I've been here. If you have one that should be on this list,
this is published as a public gist that I'm going to share on Blue Sky after the talk. So feel free to tell me
about your MCP server and I'll make sure it gets included. We have mine which is does search
documentation. We have Lexicon Gardens MCP server which actually serves lexicons to and has a describe
lexicon as well as some authenticated methods where you can actually create and invoke XRPC endpoints.
We have the AshEx has an @proto.mcp which is a local documentation MCP server. So this is one that you install locally, you run it in Python,
you perform the RAG indexing locally and you have your own local version of that. That one actually works off of the GitHub repos as opposed to the
like HTML content of the doc sites. We also have some different ones.
There's Cameron has a very extensive one that actually has a disclaimer to not use this as a REST API because it has so many tools and features.
We have the one that has the one that has the one that has the one that has the most forks which is Brian's which is actually more of like a blue sky client.

A lot of the ones that act as blue sky clients use app password authentication. So yeah, there are a bunch of really cool mcp servers out there and you can use them to attach to your agents and reduce hallucinations.
And ground your development in the official app proto documentation and official lexicons. That's it.
